main_summary.summary: Cecil J. Williams, photographer and Orangeburg, South Carolina native born 1937, recounts a career documenting the South Carolina civil rights movement from age 11 onward. Mentored by Sumter photographer E.C. Jones of Majestic Studio, Williams photographed the Briggs v. Elliott petitioners and Rev. J.A. DeLaine in Clarendon County during the case that became one of the five consolidated in Brown v. Board of Education. At 15 he became a Jet magazine correspondent. Williams documents the 1955 Orangeburg counter-boycott led by SC State student government president Fred H. Moore, who was expelled by president B.C. Turner; Thurgood Marshall's 1955 Claflin University gymnasium rally; Harvey Gantt's 1963 admission to Clemson; the 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt rejected by Mayor Clyde Fair; the 1968 All-Star Bowling Alley protests; and the Orangeburg Massacre of February 8, 1968, in which nine SC Highway Patrolmen fired on SC State students, killing three. He describes photographing a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road as a teenager, meeting Senator John F. Kennedy in January 1960, and recovering shotgun shells the morning after the massacre that the FBI later confiscated as trial evidence.
key_themes: Briggs v. Elliott and Brown v. Board of Education origins
Citizens' Council retaliation and economic coercion
South Carolina State College and Claflin University student activism
main_summary.historical_significance:
Williams's testimony is a primary-source record of the South Carolina civil rights movement from the inside, by the African American photographer who had unique access to NAACP meetings, Briggs petitioner gatherings, Thurgood Marshall's South Carolina visits, and student-led protests at SC State and Claflin. His firsthand recollection of being approached by the Selective Service after his Mayor Clyde Fair photograph appeared internationally, of having his SC State scholarship rescinded by president B.C. Turner because his Jet magazine photographs of Fred H. Moore embarrassed the administration, and of being offered an FBI position by J. Edgar Hoover's office after providing massacre shotgun shells, documents the surveillance and economic-retaliation infrastructure deployed against movement participants.

The interview is particularly valuable for its sustained argument that Clarendon County and Orangeburg represent an underrecognized origin point for the modern civil rights movement that predates and shaped the Montgomery and Greensboro narratives historians have foregrounded. Williams's documentation of the Elloree School teachers who resigned in April 1956 rather than disavow NAACP membership, and his Out of the Box in Dixie / Freedom & Justice / Orangeburg 1968 publications, provide visual and biographical material on participants almost entirely absent from the national civil rights record.

chapters.title: Opening the Interview in Orangeburg
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier and videographer John Bishop open the Civil Rights History Project interview with Cecil J. Williams at his Orangeburg, South Carolina home and studio on June 9, 2011. Williams is invited to begin with his childhood, family, and early education.

chapters.title: Family and a Lifelong Orangeburg Home
chapters.summary: Williams describes being the third child of Ethel and Cecil J. Williams, his father a self-employed tailor in Orangeburg and his mother a lifelong educator at the college, high school, and elementary levels. He explains why he chose to remain in Orangeburg despite its being a battleground for his race.

chapters.title: Becoming Aware of the Segregated Bus
chapters.summary: Williams recalls becoming conscious of race around age seven or eight, when his mother explained that they had to sit in a designated section of the Orangeburg city bus. He describes the shifting seating line and how a crowded section meant standing or not boarding at all.

chapters.title: Parallel Black and White Societies
chapters.summary: Williams describes Orangeburg as two parallel societies with separate playgrounds, schools, and movie theaters, where Black patrons sat in a designated section and could not use downtown lunch counters. He notes that interstate Greyhound and Trailways travel was likewise segregated.

chapters.title: Wilkinson High, Quick Street, Two Colleges
chapters.summary: Williams recounts attending segregated schools and graduating from Wilkinson High School, with his family living on Quick Street two blocks from South Carolina State College. He situates Orangeburg as an agricultural community midway between Columbia and Charleston, roughly forty percent African American, shaped by Claflin and South Carolina State College.

chapters.title: A Tailor Shop and a First Camera
chapters.summary: Williams explains that his father's tailoring business served an eighty percent white clientele, doing alterations for downtown stores such as Belk-Hudson, Bar Shays, and Limehouse. He recalls receiving a Kodak Baby Brownie inherited from his brother and beginning to photograph people in their Sunday finery for a dollar or two, his first photographic hustle.

chapters.title: Edisto Gardens and Saturday Movies
chapters.summary: Williams describes Edisto Gardens, open to all races, alongside contradictory barriers such as drive-in theaters closed to Black patrons. He recalls Saturdays at the Black movie theater watching serials like Captain Marvel and The Phantom, and how motion pictures, including an early viewing of Gone With the Wind, shaped his development as an aspiring photographer.

chapters.title: A Mixed-Race Household Under Jim Crow
chapters.summary: Williams reflects on a caring low-income household where his grandmother's wisdom helped raise him, and on the puzzle of his mixed-race ancestry: Indian and Black on his father's side, Black and white on his mother's. He describes the confusion this created for a young child navigating racial placement.

chapters.title: State-Sanctioned Legal Segregation by Law
chapters.summary: Williams distinguishes the legal, state-sanctioned segregation he grew up under from other forms, describing courthouse signs for white and colored water fountains and the federal, state, county, and city regulations that governed where Black citizens could attend school or ride a bus.

chapters.title: E.C. Jones and the Clarendon County Churches
chapters.summary: Born in 1937, Williams recalls connecting his camera to civil rights work around age eleven through mentor E.C. Jones of Sumter, whose Majestic Studio was called to photograph churches in Clarendon County where the Briggs, DeLaine, and Pearson families were waging the battle that became Briggs v. Elliott. Jones drove him to those Sunday church meetings.

chapters.title: Photographing for State and the NAACP
chapters.summary: Williams describes how E.C. Jones, who bought out-of-date supplies near the Sumter Air Force base, pulled him into shooting for South Carolina State College to save an eighty-mile round trip. As the NAACP and Rev. Joseph A. DeLaine called Jones into action, Williams accompanied him to make many of the Briggs v. Elliott photographs, connected through his teacher Robert Howard.

chapters.title: Thurgood Marshall Off the Train
chapters.summary: Williams recounts taking one of his first flashbulb photographs, of Thurgood Marshall stepping off the train in Charleston, after Orangeburg NAACP branch president Squire Morgan took him to the Briggs trial. He explains why movement leaders valued careful photographic documentation when the local press ignored African American news.

chapters.title: Correspondent for Jet at Fifteen
chapters.summary: Williams describes becoming an official Jet magazine correspondent at age fifteen, shooting also for the Afro-American and the Pittsburgh Courier, after Jet came to Orangeburg from Chicago to cover the developing story. He recalls the Clarendon County gatherings he and E.C. Jones photographed, where many Briggs participants, run off their land, returned for commemorations and reunions at St. Mark.

chapters.title: Clarendon County as the Catalyst
chapters.summary: Williams argues that Clarendon County and Orangeburg were the underrecognized origin of the civil rights movement, predating the 1955 Montgomery framing. He traces how the Briggs case, beginning as a bus-transportation effort, led Thurgood Marshall to see it as the template that became Brown v. Board of Education rather than suing every school district.

chapters.title: The Muted Response to Brown
chapters.summary: Asked about the 1954 Brown decision, Williams recalls the danger that surrounded it, with Briggs and DeLaine petitioners already forced to leave. He notes his mother taught under Rev. J.A. DeLaine, giving the family close ties, and explains that white control of employment put livelihoods and lives at risk for anyone associated with the effort.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision May 1954

chapters.title: Violence and Warnings to the Family
chapters.summary: Williams reflects on the extrajudicial racial violence in Orangeburg County, Elloree, and Cameron, including people who simply disappeared. He recalls white businessmen approaching his father perhaps three times, warning that outside agitators were using him, and how his mother, mindful of Rev. J.A. DeLaine's fate, spoke harshly out of fear while his parents' generation formed accommodations to survive.

chapters.title: Parents Who Bought the Cameras
chapters.summary: Williams affirms his parents never asked him to stop, instead supporting his work and buying his cameras. He recounts begging his mother during a thunderstorm, with a captive audience, for a Brownie Hawkeye he had seen in the Sears Roebuck catalog, the first camera he owned with a flash unit attached.

chapters.title: Photographing the Klan on Belleville Road
chapters.summary: Williams clarifies that he attended a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road while contributing photographs to the Orangeburg Times and Democrat. Arriving after the rally ended, he found a highway patrolman and the Grand Dragon putting out a cross burning, and the three re-erected the burned cross so he could photograph them, an image he has since lost and a danger that angered his mother.

chapters.title: Breaking Barriers as a Photographer
chapters.summary: Williams reflects that his lighter skin complexion let him break barriers for his race, recalling how at eighteen he sought membership in the South Carolina Professional Photographers of America, which had no African American members. After calling membership chairperson Dan Bohrn, he was welcomed to the Hilton Head meeting as the only Black photographer in the group photo.

chapters.title: Meeting John F. Kennedy in New York
chapters.summary: During a Claflin semester break in January 1960, Williams reads that Senator John F. Kennedy will speak at the Roosevelt Hotel. As security began to remove him from the ballroom, Kennedy and his wife arrived and stopped them, and after Williams mentioned his Claflin political science class, Kennedy cleared two seats and sat him beside Chet Huntley and David Brinkley for close-up photographs.

chapters.title: Aboard the Caroline and Kennedy's Death
chapters.summary: Williams recounts sending his photographs to Kennedy and later being the only press person allowed aboard the campaign plane Caroline when Kennedy landed in Columbia, South Carolina. He reflects on his association with a future president, on the Civil Rights Act that followed under Johnson, and on his distress at Kennedy's early death.

chapters.title: Orangeburg as a Second Battleground
chapters.summary: Returning from a break, Williams frames Orangeburg as the second of four incidents that created the modern civil rights movement. With most Briggs participants run out of Clarendon County, the movement spread to Orangeburg, a battleground rarely mentioned in history books other than his own.

chapters.title: Marshall's Rally at the Claflin Gymnasium
chapters.summary: Williams describes Thurgood Marshall returning after the Brown victory to speak at the Claflin University gymnasium, inspiring the Orangeburg community around 1955 to be among the first to send their children in a test of the Supreme Court ruling. From his inside position he photographed Marshall with leaders such as James Sulton and Matthew McCollom.

chapters.title: Preserving Marshall's Deteriorating Negatives
chapters.summary: Williams recalls photographing the packed Seabrook gymnasium rally of about 1,100 people, and how his deteriorating four-by-five and 120 negatives of Thurgood Marshall and Senator Kennedy were later scanned onto floppy disks while he worked on Freedom & Justice. He did not at first connect this Marshall with the man he had photographed years earlier in Charleston during the Briggs hearings.

chapters.title: Bearing Witness for Future Historians
chapters.summary: Williams calls his record of the unsung Elloree School teachers his defining moment as a photographer, with their pictures in no other publication. He argues the movement will hold greater importance fifty to a hundred years from now, having established America's integrity before a watching world and brought morality, freedom, justice, and equality that made the country better for everyone.

chapters.title: Fred H. Moore and the Counter-Boycott
chapters.summary: Williams begins the story of his rescinded scholarship by explaining the pressures on state-funded South Carolina State College versus Methodist-rooted Claflin. He describes how student leader Fred H. Moore was expelled for leading students who supported the Orangeburg counter-boycott, after parents petitioned School District 5 and white pressure whittled the signers from around twenty down to nine.

chapters.title: A Rescinded Scholarship, a Matching Offer
chapters.summary: Williams describes the Citizens' Council pressuring petition supporters and the Black counter-boycott of selected merchants, including the Coca-Cola plant owner. After president B.C. Turner, embarrassed by his Jet photographs of Fred H. Moore, had Harold Crawford rescind his South Carolina State College scholarship, Claflin's new president Dr. H.V. Manning matched it exactly, and Williams enrolled to study art and photography.

chapters.title: A Melting Pot of Student Activism
chapters.summary: Williams describes Orangeburg's two African American colleges, South Carolina State and Claflin, drawing students from across the state and nation into a melting pot of activism. He explains that students, unlike their parents, wanted the Supreme Court ruling realized now, placing Orangeburg at the epicenter of student activism even before Greensboro.

chapters.title: Erasing the Petition by Pressure
chapters.summary: Williams confirms that the Citizens' Council moved quickly because the parents' petition came soon after Brown, seeking to erase every name so the petition would become moot. He recalls that his art teacher Arthur Rose of Charleston was one signer, often turning art education classes into discussions of the movement he was willing to give his life for.

chapters.title: The Elloree Teachers Resign
chapters.summary: Williams describes South Carolina in turmoil after Brown, with a school in Cameron closing and the all-white legislature requiring teachers to declare NAACP membership before reemployment. The Elloree elementary teachers resigned in April 1956 rather than sign, and principal Mr. Davis called Williams to photograph them, an image that spanned two pages and became Jet's largest published picture at the time.

chapters.title: The Cost the Teachers Paid
chapters.summary: Williams describes the NAACP being branded communist-inspired, making membership risky in white-controlled employment. He laments that the Elloree teachers' positions were quickly filled by others outside the movement, that many never regained employment in South Carolina, and that fewer than three remain alive, representing the heroism of countless nameless grassroots people whose actions he has tried to record.

chapters.title: A Front Seat to History on Boulevard
chapters.summary: Newly married after graduating in 1960, Williams explains how a Black business drew support from Black citizens, with South Carolina State and Claflin as main contractors for yearbooks. His studio on Boulevard, opposite the campuses and alongside other Black businesses, gave him a front-row view as marches passed his door, what he calls his front seat to history.

chapters.title: A Continuous Orangeburg Movement
chapters.summary: Williams contrasts the episodic way history books record civil rights incidents with Orangeburg's continuous protest from about 1947 through 1968, attributing this steadfastness to citizens close to the Clarendon County situation. He points to African American soldiers returning from the world's theaters only to be treated as second-class citizens, naming James Sulton as a postwar Orangeburg leader.

chapters.title: Harvey Gantt's Admission to Clemson
chapters.summary: Williams recounts Harvey Gantt of Charleston winning admission to Clemson around 1963 through Matthew J. Perry Jr. and Constance Baker Motley after a District Court order. He covered the event exclusively for Jet magazine and the Black press as one of only three African Americans on the campus that morning, amid awareness that violence could erupt as it had for James Meredith.

chapters.title: A Closed Campus and a Denied Letter
chapters.summary: Williams explains that authorities closed the Clemson campus and ordered Black employees, faculty, and students away, leaving only Gantt, reporters, Strom Thurmond, and police. Traveling in a convoy with Perry and Motley, he reflects that Gantt achieved a dream he once held, recalling his own ambitions toward architecture and a letter to Governor Strom Thurmond about out-of-state tuition that went unanswered.

chapters.title: Setting the Pace and Mourning Kennedy
chapters.summary: Williams notes that Gantt's quiet admission set the pace for integrating Clemson and the University of South Carolina, where African Americans had attended during Reconstruction. Asked about Kennedy's 1963 assassination, he recalls a community, Black and white, deeply shaken, with a silent ceremony at State College, crediting Kennedy with setting the mid-1960s Civil Rights Act in motion under Johnson.

chapters.title: Turned Away at St. Paul Methodist
chapters.summary: Williams describes his 1961 photograph of friends Dorothy Vann and Emmanuel Hixson, students from out of state, being turned away from a downtown Orangeburg church. Tipped off, he ran up the steps as Mayor Clyde Fair, a member of St. Paul Methodist Church, told the students it was a segregated church and to worship with their own people.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

chapters.title: The Photograph That Circled the World
chapters.summary: Williams recounts selling the church photograph to the Associated Press in Columbia for thirty-five dollars. The next day it ran worldwide, including front pages in Germany, the Washington Star, and the New York Times, showing a southern mayor refusing a Black worshipper and revealing Sunday morning as one of the most segregated times in America.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

chapters.title: The Selective Service Drafts Him
chapters.summary: About a month later, Williams, then pursuing his master's at South Carolina State College with a deferment, is summoned to the Selective Service office on Doyle Street, where a clipping of his church photograph floated to the floor from his file. Given thirty days, he joins the Air Force, closes his studio, is injured in flight training, and returns to Orangeburg to rebuild.

chapters.title: Landmarks and an Attempted Bribe
chapters.summary: Mosnier lists the landmarks Williams documented, including 1962 State Capitol protests and the 1963 University of South Carolina desegregation. Williams recounts being approached twice by the Citizens' Council, including a man known only as Jack who tried to buy his photograph of a meeting at Trinity AME Church, an attempted bribe he refused and reported to Rev. Matthew McCollom.

chapters.title: Naming Agitators, Tokenism, Resistance
chapters.summary: Williams counters the press framing of outside agitators, communists, and infiltrators, insisting the movement was an African American one that Black citizens started. Asked how Orangeburg changed after the 1964 and 1965 legislation, he describes a period of tokenism and Black hiring, with changes appreciated yet pockets of resistance remaining in segregated churches, bowling alleys, and restaurants.

chapters.title: The All-Star Bowling Alley Protest
chapters.summary: Williams describes the All-Star Bowling Alley on Russell Street with its small white-only sign, which students passed walking uptown from South Carolina State and Claflin. After students who had been driving to Columbia to bowl were arrested, others joined the protest beginning around February 6, and as the yearbook photographer Williams was present among 250 to 300 students facing highway patrolmen and police.

chapters.title: A Broken Window and a Beating
chapters.summary: Pressed against a vacant store's glass in the packed strip mall, a student broke a window, and police raised their clubs and began hitting people as if it were intentional, sending everyone running. Fleeing toward campus with his twin-lens camera, Williams looked back near Sutcliffe's furniture store to see two city policemen beat a fallen female student on the head, three days before the Orangeburg Massacre.

chapters.title: Nine Patrolmen Fire on Students
chapters.summary: On the night of the massacre Williams returns from Columbia but cannot reach State College, finding police blocking College Avenue, an obstruction he later realized probably saved his life. After students built a bonfire, nine highway patrolmen crept onto campus and fired shotguns into a crowd of about 250, killing three and wounding about twenty-seven, almost all shot from the back.

chapters.title: Recovering the Shotgun Shells
chapters.summary: Citing Jack Bass's book The Orangeburg Massacre and his own Orangeburg 1968, Williams recounts driving to the campus on a freezing, overcast morning to find the maintenance crew clearing debris where the students were killed. He gathered shotgun shells into his pockets and posed a photograph of a student holding the seven, eight, or nine shells, which appeared in Time, Newsweek, and papers across the United States.

chapters.title: Evidence Confiscated, Hoover's Offer Refused
chapters.summary: Williams explains that the FBI confiscated the shells, later used at the Florence, South Carolina trial where the nine patrolmen were found not guilty. The agent who took them relayed that J. Edgar Hoover had asked whether Williams wanted to become an FBI agent, an offer he refused, suspecting they wanted to make an informant of him, before warning local leaders McCollom, Sulton, and Oscar Butler.

chapters.title: Defenseless, Forgotten, and Finney's Protest
chapters.summary: Williams insists the killings were a massacre because the students were defenseless and unarmed, far less known than the Kent State Massacre. Mosnier notes that Ernest A. Finney Jr., then head of the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission, and another member resigned in 1969 over the lack of prosecutorial effort, which Williams confirms before a break.

chapters.title: The Pink Palace and 388 Arrests
chapters.summary: Williams turns to his books and the Pink Palace, the city police jail where he was jailed twice but bailed out by the NAACP so he could keep photographing. He describes a front-page image of the stockade where 388 demonstrators were arrested in 1960, so many that penitentiary buses were called from Columbia, with soaked students funneled behind the jail in the wake of the Greensboro sit-ins.

chapters.title: Well-Dressed Students and a Prayer Demonstration
chapters.summary: Williams reflects on how well-dressed and orderly the demonstrators were even before Martin Luther King's call for nonviolent resistance. He shows a photograph of a 1963 downtown Orangeburg demonstration of 3,000 people, including his own mother, who prayed for peace, opportunity, and the employment of Black citizens in a town with almost no Black police, council members, or county officials.

chapters.title: The Books and Unsung Heroes
chapters.summary: Williams describes Out of the Box in Dixie as a top-quality record of the movement's dignity and unsung heroes, with almost no images of Martin Luther King, Rosa Parks, or Jesse Jackson, running from Clarendon County through 2008 or 2009. He notes Freedom & Justice and The Orangeburg Massacre, the latter carrying interviews with those who were shot.
keywords: Out of the Box in Dixie
related_events: publication of Out of the Box in Dixie

chapters.title: John Stroman, Cleveland Sellers, and Closing
chapters.summary: Williams describes John Stroman, whom authorities tried to scapegoat, and Cleveland Sellers, the only person jailed in connection with the Orangeburg Massacre, actually for the February 6 bowling alley incident, who later became president of Voorhees College. Mosnier thanks Williams, and the session ends with the standard Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC sign-off.

loc_video.caption: Cecil J. Williams oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Orangeburg, South Carolina, 2011 June 09
